From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [John E. Newton](/w/index.php?title=John_E._Newton&redirect=no "John E. Newton"))

British trade unionist

"John E. Newton" redirects here. For the Nebraska judge, see [John E. Newton (judge)](/wiki/John_E._Newton_(judge) "John E. Newton (judge)").

**John Edward Newton** (16 June 1908[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) – 4 January 1993)[[3]](#cite_note-3) was a [British](/wiki/British_people "British people") [trade unionist](/wiki/Trade_unionist "Trade unionist").

Born in [Lofthouse](/wiki/Lofthouse,_West_Yorkshire "Lofthouse, West Yorkshire") in Yorkshire,[[4]](#cite_note-tuc-4) Newton began work in tailoring at the age of 21.[[5]](#cite_note-guardian-5) He won awards for his cutting and design skills, but increasingly came to focus on trade unionism.[[4]](#cite_note-tuc-4)

Newton was elected as the president of the [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough "Middlesbrough") branch of the [National Union of Tailors and Garment Workers](/wiki/National_Union_of_Tailors_and_Garment_Workers "National Union of Tailors and Garment Workers") (NUTGW) in 1939, he was elected to the union's executive council in 1943, then became assistant regional organiser for the North of England in 1947 and then regional organiser in 1948.[[4]](#cite_note-tuc-4) In 1952, he was elected as the union's general secretary, taking over from [Anne Loughlin](/wiki/Anne_Loughlin "Anne Loughlin") the following year.[[5]](#cite_note-guardian-5) He also served as president of the [International Textile and Garment Workers' Federation](/wiki/International_Textile_and_Garment_Workers%27_Federation "International Textile and Garment Workers' Federation") from its formation, then as the first president of the new [International Textile, Garment and Leather Workers' Federation](/wiki/International_Textile,_Garment_and_Leather_Workers%27_Federation "International Textile, Garment and Leather Workers' Federation"), standing down in 1972.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Newton resigned as secretary of the NUTGW in 1968, citing the pressure of work.[[7]](#cite_note-7) He also served on the executive council of the [Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Trades_Union_Congress "Trades Union Congress") (TUC), and was [President of the TUC](/wiki/President_of_the_TUC "President of the TUC") in 1969.
